metadata.title: Populist alone, but harmless together? Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID and ECR on Facebook

abstract: Right-wing populist parties are highly successful in online communication and have made electoral gains in a wide range of European countries. In the European Parliament, until the recent European elections in 2024, the majority of them were distributed between the two party alliances and parliamentary groups Identity and Democracy (ID) and European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR). To analyse their communicative strategies, we collected all Facebook posts of the party and parliamentary group accounts of ID and ECR (N = 616) within one year (08/ 21-08/22). Subsequently, structural topic modelling (STM), sentiment analysis and manual content analysis were applied to determine their communication strategies. Overall, the results indicate that ECR and ID are communicating rather moderately online. However, while ECR differs significantly from its national member parties, ID seems to reproduce right-wing populist narratives. Overall, this paper argues for a stronger focus on European party alliances, not only national parties, when researching right-wing populist communication in the EU.

sections.paper_id: Peters and Schlußmeier - 2025 - Populist alone, but harmless together Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID
paragraphs: Over the past decade, right-wing populist parties and actors have made electoral gains in a variety of European countries (Moffitt, 2016;Silver, 2022), and at the transnational level, notably so in the past elections to the European Parliament. In 2019, right-wing populist MEPs were able to win 'between a quarter and a third of the EP's seats' (Mudde, 2019: 20). Until the recent European election in 2024, the majority of these were divided between the two EP groups and European party alliances, Identity and Democracy (ID) and European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR). As EP groups, ID and ECR consisted of a number of national parties that have mostly been at the centre of previous research on right-wing populist actors: for example, the AfD in Germany (Gründl, 2022;Puschmann et al., 2020), the Rassemblement National in France (Carral et al., 2023) or Lega in Italy (Berti and Loner, 2021). Even when populism in 'Europe' is studied, the focus is primarily on national parties and actors (e.g. Aalberg et al., 2017;Alonso-Muñoz and Casero-Ripollés, 2020), but not on the parties and groups of the European Parliament. This may also be related to the fact that the current development is primarily understood as national populism (Eatwell and Goodwin, 2018). However, the networking of right-wing populists and extreme right-wing actors and parties in Europe suggests that international and transnational populism is emerging as well (Kuyper and Moffitt, 2020). In this context, it is particularly interesting to look at the European parties, as these are institutionalised political organisations of international right-wing populism. Beyond that, with the Treaty of Lisbon, the powers of the EU Parliament have been significantly expanded (e.g. as a legislator), meaning that European party alliances and especially the EP groups became more influential in EU politics as well (Kantola et al., 2022: 2). With the rise of right-wing populism at the European level and the increasing importance of the EU Parliament, it becomes more relevant to analyse these forms of institutionalised right-wing populism. Therefore, in this article, inter-and transnational populism are examined in terms of the associated communicative strategies of ID and ECR as party alliances on the European level. While other right-wing populist parties such as, for example, the Hungarian Fidesz, the Italian Five Stars Movement or the Dutch Forum for Democracy are also members in the European Parliament, they are first and foremost national parties and will therefore not be regarded in this article. The primary interest is on the one hand to compare the social media communication of ID and ECR with each other. On the other hand, we contrast both of them with their member parties. While we collected and analysed the social media data for ID and ECR ourselves, we used the results of previously published studies to discuss the differences and similarities with the national parties.

(Trans-)National conceptions of and digital implications for right-wing populism in Europe the case of populism it is mainly about 'the people' (Mudde, 2004: 544). As a 'thin' construct, populism is often combined with other ideologies. For instance, when it comes to right-wing populism, classic populist communication strategies like elite blaming are now combined with nativism:

1. Elite blaming: This refers to an antagonism between 'the pure people' and 'the corrupt elite', which takes place within a society. In addition to political elites, this includes institutions and groups from the fields of 'economics, law, or media' (Engesser et al., 2017a(Engesser et al., : 1117)). The right-wing populist's main accusation against the elites is that they have cheated the general population out of their power (Engesser et al., 2017b(Engesser et al., : 1281)). In contrast, populists not only claim to be part of the population, whose power they are trying to regain, but they portray themselves as the only 'true representatives of the people' (Engesser et al., 2017a(Engesser et al., : 1116)). 2. Nativism: Nativism primarily consists of the assignment of groups of people to a geographical location where they are 'natives' (Mudde, 2007: 18) and which should constitute their place of residence. Members within such a population then form an ingroup, non-members an outgroup (Mudde, 2007: 63). The ingroup is 'based on the belief in a shared European culture (or civilization)' (Mudde, 2007: 182) which has to be defended from the outgroup (Mudde, 2007). However, ingroup and outgroup are not only determined geographically, the outgroup also includes migrants, ethnic minorities, queer people, feminists (Mudde, 2007: 64-68) and religious groups (Engesser et al., 2017a(Engesser et al., : 1119)).

These two antagonisms are often associated with nationalism (Eatwell and Goodwin, 2018;Flew and Iosifidis, 2020), partly because the nation is the central reference category within right-wing populist concepts. According to Mudde (2007: 17f), the national population is the essential ingroup that needs to be protected from the outside (e.g. through migration) and the inside (from elites), as previously described. Even though being conceptualised in connection with nation-states, recent work has argued for broadening the perspective of populism and to include inter-and transnational elements (Kuyper and Moffitt, 2020;McDonnell and Werner, 2019).
Inter-and transnational right-wing populism in Europe: ID and ECR

The existence of inter-and transnational populism seems counterintuitive at first glance, as globalisation is regarded to be a driver of national populism (Rodrik, 2021). For instance, potential new voter groups of right-wing populist parties are considered to be losers of globalisation (Steiner et al., 2024). Therefore, national populist parties often reject transnational actors as elites or are at least sceptical of them. In the European context, national populist actors have often equated the EU with a cosmopolitan elite that threatens the sovereignty of the nation (Csehi and Zgut, 2020). This rhetoric was particularly successful in the context of Brexit by UKIP and the Brexit Party (now Reform UK) (Calhoun, 2017;Norris and Inglehart, 2019). However, recent research has emphasised that globalisation and populism are not necessarily mutually exclusive.

For instance, globalisation is still considered the 'common enemy' (Caiani, 2018: 407) by right-wing populist parties at the European level. Therefore, the question arises as to what constitutes inter-and transnational populism. According to Kuyper and Moffitt, international populism occurs when national populist actors 'seek to construct alliances, allegiances, and affinities with populists in other nation-states […], without claiming to represent "the people" of those other states ' (2020: 32). Transnational populism, on the other hand, takes place when 'populists make representative claims on behalf of "the people" that explicitly stretch beyond the borders of a single nation-state' (Kuyper and Moffitt, 2020: 33). In this respect, the central populist antagonism of 'the people' and 'the elite' does not necessarily have to refer to nation-states, but can also be linked to regional, transnational or global groupings. Institutional structures are an important condition for the constitution of inter-and transnational populism. In the European context, for example, the institutional structure of the EU and the European Parliament functions as 'an avenue for representatives to formally seek authorisation from the transnational people they claim to represent' (Kuyper and Moffitt, 2020: 40).

Political groups in the European Parliament (EP) are constituted after EU elections. At least 23 MEPs from at least seven member states with similar political positions are required to form a group in the EP (Kantola et al., 2022: 4). The size of the EP groups is highly relevant, 'because it impacts the relative political power of the groups in EP decision-and policy-making' (Kantola et al., 2022: 5). The right-wing populist parties in the EU Parliament were ID and ECR, which McDonnell and Werner classify as 'radical right populist ' (2019: 216). Both formed the respective EP groups of the same name with national party delegations. Even if the so-called Europarties are closely related to the EP groups and often have a large overlap in terms of their national member parties, they cannot be considered identical (Ahrens and Kantola, 2022: 45). The ECR group was founded in 2009 by the British UK Conservatives, with the aim of reforming the EU (Leruth, 2017: 391). The ID party emerged in 2019 and was founded by the Danish People's Party, the Finns Party and the Alternative for Germany, which had previously been members of ECR (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 58-61). As an amalgamation of national parties (see Table 1), both can be assigned to the category of international populism (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 34). While conservative parties in the EU Parliament tend to devote themselves to issues such as 'the economy, taxation and government spending, national security, agriculture and forestry' (Haßler et al., 2020: 91), right-wing populist parties address topics such as 'migration, refugees, crimes of immigrants, political radicalism, and religious fanaticism' (Haßler et al., 2020). Since immigration is perceived 'first and foremost as a European issue' (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 17), right-wing parties hope to be more successful in tackling migration at the European level. This shift towards a common policy is also informed by the fact that the population of European countries largely supports the EU (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 220). Right-wing populists thus often move away from an anti-EU stance in their agenda in order to tie in with the political centre and at the same time make use of the funding the EU provides (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 27). As euroscepticism offers few advantages for right-wing populist parties at the European level, they only criticise individual elements of the EU or are 'skeptical about the current direction of the EU' (Mudde, 2007: 164) instead. For right-wing populist parties, participation in the parliamentary group rather serves to gain prestige at the political level. This is because EP groups do not primarily aim to win voters or to enthuse their own supporters (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 183) which is evident from the fact that national parties barely mention their EP groups (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 179). Since only national and not European parties are elected, Group and party: Sweden Democrats a Due to changes in party and group affiliation, the national parties listed here do not necessarily correspond to the parties that were members of ID or ECR at the time of the survey. Notably, the German AfD has since been removed from the ID group.

campaigning and reporting on European elections takes place predominantly at the national level (Della Porta, 2022: 52;Haßler et al., 2020: 92), making a strong connection between voters and EP parties superfluous. In this way, the strategies of right-wing populists at the European level differ from those of national parties. National right-wing populist parties consider themselves as anti-establishment and protest parties opposing the political centre (Rydgren, 2005: 424).

At the European level, however, it can be assumed that one goal of EU right-wing populist parties at present is to normalise themselves and lose their radical image (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 200). Therefore, they increasingly aim to associate with the political centre-right (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 44). The empirical part of this work will therefore also look closely at the extent to which these assumed strategies are manifested in the parties' social media communication.

This shift towards a common policy is also informed by the fact that the population of European countries largely supports the EU (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 220). Right-wing populists thus often move away from an anti-EU stance in their agenda in order to tie in with the political centre and at the same time make use of the funding the EU provides (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 27). As euroscepticism offers few advantages for right-wing populist parties at the European level, they only criticise individual elements of the EU or are 'skeptical about the current direction of the EU' (Mudde, 2007: 164) instead. For right-wing populist parties, participation in the parliamentary group rather serves to gain prestige at the political level. This is because EP groups do not primarily aim to win voters or to enthuse their own supporters (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 183) which is evident from the fact that national parties barely mention their EP groups (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 179). Since only national and not European parties are elected, Group and party: Sweden Democrats a Due to changes in party and group affiliation, the national parties listed here do not necessarily correspond to the parties that were members of ID or ECR at the time of the survey. Notably, the German AfD has since been removed from the ID group.

campaigning and reporting on European elections takes place predominantly at the national level (Della Porta, 2022: 52;Haßler et al., 2020: 92), making a strong connection between voters and EP parties superfluous. In this way, the strategies of right-wing populists at the European level differ from those of national parties. National right-wing populist parties consider themselves as anti-establishment and protest parties opposing the political centre (Rydgren, 2005: 424).

At the European level, however, it can be assumed that one goal of EU right-wing populist parties at present is to normalise themselves and lose their radical image (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 200). Therefore, they increasingly aim to associate with the political centre-right (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 44). The empirical part of this work will therefore also look closely at the extent to which these assumed strategies are manifested in the parties' social media communication.
sections.preview_head: Over the past decade, right-wing populist parties and actors have made electoral gains in a variety of European countries (Moffitt, 2016;Silver, 2022), and at the transnational level, notably so in the past elections to the European Parliament. In 2019, right-wing populist MEPs were able to win 'between a quarter and a third of the EP's seats' (Mudde, 2019: 20). Until the recent European election in 2024, the majority of these were divided between the two EP groups and European party alliances, Identity and Democracy (ID) and European Conservatives and Reformists (ECR).

sections.paper_id: Peters and Schlußmeier - 2025 - Populist alone, but harmless together Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID
sections.header_norm: right wing populism in social media
paragraphs: Right-wing populist parties have been not only successful in recent elections but also on social media where they appeal to a large audience (Gil de Zúñiga et al., 2020;Puschmann et al., 2020: 242). In the course of a new digital transformation of the public sphere (Habermas, 2022), right-wing populists are bypassing the traditional media (television, newspapers, etc.) and are able to become agenda-setters themselves. The appeal of social media lies in the fact that accounts are difficult to censor (Mudde, 2007: 259), while traditional media exercises a 'watchdog' function (Ernst et al., 2019: 5) and serves as a gatekeeper (Haßler et al., 2020: 89).

The connection between populism and social media has already been widely studied (Boulianne et al., 2020;Engesser et al., 2017a;Ernst et al., 2019;Postill, 2018). What appears to be certain is the affordances of digital platforms accommodating the communication patterns of populist actors. For example, recent studies indicate that negative campaigning, dramatisation and, at least in the 2014 European elections, populist content is associated with increased user engagement (Klinger et al., 2023). User engagement, in turn, is a central unit when it comes to the algorithmic prioritisation of content on digital platforms, for example in the form of suggested trends (van Dijck et al., 2018). Furthermore, the targeted dissemination of disinformation is also associated with populist actors using digital media (Corbu and Negrea-Busuioc, 2020). In this context, right-wing populist actors often make use of rather emotionalised communication, in particular, a negative presentation of certain issues (Alonso-Muñoz and Casero-Ripollés, 2023; Engesser et al., 2017bEngesser et al., : 1285)). This was evident, for example, in a study of Twitter accounts of the party leaders of Rassemblement National, FPÖ, AfD, Vox and Chega! (Rivas- de-Roca et al., 2022: 33).

A large number of empirical studies indicate that national populist parties use communication strategies of right-wing populism in social media: elite blaming and nativism. Thus, right-wing populist actors in social media turn particularly strongly against the mainstream media (Engesser et al., 2017a), by whom they feel unrepresented and whom they accuse of being part of a social elite (Ellinas, 2018: 269). Moreover, a number of studies provide evidence of the negative portrayal of the groups that define right-wing populist parties as outgroups. Carral et al. show, for example, that posts by the Rassemblement National on the topic of immigration are predominantly portrayed negatively on Twitter, as are the EU and various pieces of legislation, while their own party is shown positively (2023: 10). Pfetsch et al. (2023) come to similar conclusions. In their analysis of the Facebook campaigns of six national European parties, they found that all parties except the Polish Prawo i Sprawiedliwos ć́f requently mentioned the topic of immigration. They also assessed that those parties which were members of ID criticised the ruling parties as elites while they themselves formed the political opposition (Pfetsch et al., 2023: 7f). Furthermore, the study by Heft et al. revealed radical right-wing parties on Facebook portraying their political opponents as elites as well, although it differs between the parties analysed who exactly represents the opposing group (2023: 33f).

sections.paper_id: Peters and Schlußmeier - 2025 - Populist alone, but harmless together Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID
sections.header_raw: ID and ECR on social media: Research questions
sections.header_norm: id and ecr on social media research questions
paragraphs: The aim of this research is to analyse the social media communication of the European parties ID and ECR as an example of inter-and/or transnational populism. The focus of the study will therefore be on classic populist communication strategies (Weyland, 2001), rather than the populist ideology as defined by Mudde (2004Mudde ( , 2007) ) or as a style (Jagers and Walgrave, 2007) (see also Engesser et al., 2017bEngesser et al., : 1280)). These populist forms of communication will be analysed on two different levels: First in terms of the content, and second in terms of the sentiments. With regard to content, the state of research has highlighted national right-wing populist parties in Europe frequently applying the strategies of elite blaming and nativism. With regard to sentiments, right-wing populist groups often use a negative tone in their social media posts (Engesser et al., 2017b(Engesser et al., : 1285;;Hameleers et al., 2021: 246). This is also related to the fact that negative campaigning is associated with increased user engagement (Klinger et al., 2023).

As classified right-wing populist parties, it seems natural to assume ID and ECR correspond to right-wing populist modes of communication. However, in the previous chapter, it was emphasised that the European right-wing populist party alliances are special types of organisations whose primary interests and communication do not necessarily correspond to those of their national member parties. It became rather clear that ID and ECR are interested in becoming more compatible with less radical party alliances at the EU level and normalising themselves as a result. A devaluation of other parties as 'corrupt elites' could therefore be avoided. The dimension of nativism, for example through a negative portrayal of migrants and refugees, seems more likely, because in this way a native European population is constructed as an ingroup. Even if ID and ECR are expected to use right-wing populist strategies, it can also be assumed that they will do so in a more moderate manner. Of course, this applies not only to the content but also to the tone of the posts. However, ID and ECR cannot be regarded as equivalent. Rather, the two parties do have relevant differences. For example, such was based on conflicts in the course of the founding of ID, which were accompanied by a disengagement of certain parties from ECR. It was also pointed out in the research that ECR in particular pursued the strategy of presenting itself as more moderate. For instance, an important reason for joining ECR was the positive association with the UK Conservatives, which was to ensure respectability at the national level (McDonnell and Werner, 2019: 81, 204). ID, on the other hand, seems to be establishing itself as the most radical European party. In 2022, Nicola Procaccini, Vice President of the ECR, ruled out to merge with the ID due to political differences (Pascale, 2022). While the ECR, for example, supported the sanctions against Russia in the context of the war in Ukraine, the majority of the ID rejected them (Pascale, 2022). Additionally, ID's positioning in the Ukraine-Russia war resulted in the far-right Finns Party leaving ID and joining the ECR in the EU Parliament (Camut, 2023). Accordingly, it can be assumed that in a direct comparison ID communicates right-wing populist narratives more strongly than ECR. Against the backdrop of these considerations, two main research questions were formulated: RQ1: Do ECR and ID apply the right-wing populist communication strategies used by their national member parties? RQ2: Do ECR and ID differ in terms of communicating right-wing populist communication strategies?

sections.paper_id: Peters and Schlußmeier - 2025 - Populist alone, but harmless together Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID
paragraphs: To pursue these questions, all Facebook posts from the ID and ECR party and group accounts were collected from 23/08/2021 to 22/08/2022 with N = 616 (see Table 2). These dates marked the first day after the summer break of the European Parliament, so they provided a logical framework for the analysis. Only reposts from other accounts that did not contain additional text were excluded. Facebook was chosen for this study as ECR and ID have the largest followings on this platform compared to their other social media accounts.

To analyse both the content and the sentiment of the postings, a variety of methodological techniques were applied. First, to identify which topics are communicated by ID and ECR on Facebook, Structural Topic Modelling was used with the STM package in R (Roberts et al., 2019). STM is an unsupervised machine learning technique that automatically models a predetermined number of latent topics. It was chosen as a method as it was used by a large number of the most relevant studies (Heft et al., 2023;Pfetsch et al., 2023) to identify communication strategies of national right-wing European parties and to increase the comparability with them. The quanteda-package in R was used to conduct the common preprocessing steps like removing stopwords, punctuations, symbols, URLs and numbers. In addition, words with less than three characters were removed and a lemmatisation was carried out. As STM has not been suitable for multilingual datasets so far, all non-English posts (n = 44) were (automatically) translated into English. However, single posts that were translated into multiple languages were only included once here. Together with all posts without textual content, 18 posts were excluded from the analysis and consequently, 598 posts were analysed. Since our interest is primarily focused on the comparison between ID and ECR, we decided to calculate separate STMs for both partiesone for ID with 263 posts and one for ECR with 335 postsand not a joint model. This is due to the fact that we wanted to identify topics for both parties independently of each other, especially as ECR generally published more posts and therefore accounts for a larger proportion of the total data set. To determine the number of topics, we followed the procedure proposed by Maier et al. (2018). First, three models were calculated for ID and three for ECR with 5, 10 and 15 topics each. The relevant parameters semantic coherence (Mimno et al., 2011) and exclusivity were included in the selection process. In addition, the topics were evaluated independently by two researchers with regard to both the 10 most relevant words and the 20 most important documents (posts) of a topic. All STM solutions and every topic were then discussed, similarities and deviations were identified and the topics were labelled. Topics that were deemed invalid by the two researchers were excluded from further analysis. This only applied to one topic of the ECR, which was considered not interpretable in content by the experts independently of each other. On this basis, the 10-topic solution was chosen for both the ID and ECR models (see Table 3). As a high degree of validation is recommended as crucial for the methodological quality in the use of topic modelling (Maier et al., 2018), we also manually analysed the content of the postings. A major advantage of this strategy is the inclusion and analysis of image and video content, as only the textual content was used in the STM. To study the tonality of the contributions, two coders manually coded all postings independently of each other on a scale of positive, neutral, and negative values. With a Krippendorff's Alpha of 0.86, a high degree of intercoder reliability could be reached. In addition, we applied a dictionary-based approach using Nielsen's (2011) AFINN sentiment lexicon, implemented in the Syuzhet package (Jockers, 2023) in R. We decided in advance to use this dictionary as it was designed specifically for social media content. The AFINN scale ranges from -5 to +5. We later used the standardisation function of the Syuzhet package to rescale the original AFINN scores. Note: The values of the solution with 10 topics are marked in bold, as this model was chosen for the analysis.

Documents with a sentiment score greater than 0 were recoded as positive (1) and with less than 0 as negative (-1). If the sentiment score was 0, the respective document was evaluated as neutral (0). This was done to emphasize the different frequencies of using positive and negative connoted words by the two parties. We have also corrected the original AFINN sentiment scores for the word count of the posts. The presentation of results will therefore contain the findings from topic modelling, sentiment analysis and their illustration using individual examples from the manual analysis.

The relevant parameters semantic coherence (Mimno et al., 2011) and exclusivity were included in the selection process. In addition, the topics were evaluated independently by two researchers with regard to both the 10 most relevant words and the 20 most important documents (posts) of a topic. All STM solutions and every topic were then discussed, similarities and deviations were identified and the topics were labelled. Topics that were deemed invalid by the two researchers were excluded from further analysis. This only applied to one topic of the ECR, which was considered not interpretable in content by the experts independently of each other. On this basis, the 10-topic solution was chosen for both the ID and ECR models (see Table 3). As a high degree of validation is recommended as crucial for the methodological quality in the use of topic modelling (Maier et al., 2018), we also manually analysed the content of the postings. A major advantage of this strategy is the inclusion and analysis of image and video content, as only the textual content was used in the STM. To study the tonality of the contributions, two coders manually coded all postings independently of each other on a scale of positive, neutral, and negative values. With a Krippendorff's Alpha of 0.86, a high degree of intercoder reliability could be reached. In addition, we applied a dictionary-based approach using Nielsen's (2011) AFINN sentiment lexicon, implemented in the Syuzhet package (Jockers, 2023) in R. We decided in advance to use this dictionary as it was designed specifically for social media content. The AFINN scale ranges from -5 to +5. We later used the standardisation function of the Syuzhet package to rescale the original AFINN scores. Note: The values of the solution with 10 topics are marked in bold, as this model was chosen for the analysis.

Documents with a sentiment score greater than 0 were recoded as positive (1) and with less than 0 as negative (-1). If the sentiment score was 0, the respective document was evaluated as neutral (0). This was done to emphasize the different frequencies of using positive and negative connoted words by the two parties. We have also corrected the original AFINN sentiment scores for the word count of the posts. The presentation of results will therefore contain the findings from topic modelling, sentiment analysis and their illustration using individual examples from the manual analysis.

sections.paper_id: Peters and Schlußmeier - 2025 - Populist alone, but harmless together Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID
paragraphs: The results of the STM show that neither immigration (nativism) nor elite blaming are among the top three topics for both parties. Rather, administrative and self-referential issues are in the foreground in both data sets. In the case of ID, these are topics such as 'plenary sessions', the 'ID group' or 'ID party' itself (see Table 4). In addition, there are topics on EP members and events. Similar subjects can also be identified for the ECR (see Table 5). Here, the focus is on the ECR group in the European Parliament, its EP members and its own events and conferences as well. In addition, the future of the EU, current events and the war in Ukraine are relevant topics for ECR. For both parties, the clear reference to Europe and the EU is noticeable. Especially in the ECR, but also in ID, words like europe and european are among the most important terms of a variety of topics. This points to ID and ECR constructing Europe and the European population as an ingroup, which is a classic feature of transnational populism. For example, ID group member Harald Vilimsky called for the preservation of a 'Europe that the Founding Fathers always had in mind' (Figure 1), referring to a mythical past which is a common strategy of the radical right (Rydgren, 2018: 1). However, the 'European' is often tied back to national identities, singularities and sovereignity, for example when the ID party account cites Maximilian Krauss, chairman of the FPÖ youth organisation, who states that "European identity must be based on national pride". As a consequence, the importance, if not the primacy, of the nation-state is thematised at the same time, which in turn is more attributable to international populism (Kuyper and Moffitt, 2020). Both ECR and ID regularly write contributions and congratulations on national holidays and independence days. The ECR's national focus is also evident in the topic 'Europe of nations', while the ID's focus is particularly evident in the topic of France. The latter topic might also refer to the prominent role of the Rassemblement National in the ID party and group.

One of the ID topics modelled included social media. Portraying the media as an enemy of the 'pure people' is also a common narrative of right-wing populist parties (Haßler et al., 2020: 89). In this analysis though, only social media campaigns were mentioned. The manual evaluation of the posts revealed that traditional mass media was barely referred to at all, with only one post which even assessed mass media favourably. While only the commonalities between ID and ECR have been described so far, there are clear differences between both, especially referring to populist narratives. In this regard, ECR focuses neither on migration nor on elite blaming. For ID, on the other hand, two topics on migration were modelled. With a prevalence of 10.75% 'Immigration I' is rather concerned with internal party discourses such as the so-called 'immigration forum', while 'Immigration II' addresses particularly the protection of (Polish) EU borders with a prevalence of 06.94%. However, the migration topics do include refugees from the war in Ukraine.
For ID, a topic was modelled that can be understood as a preliminary stage of elite blaming. The topic is particularly centred around the French President Emmanuel Macron (Figure 2).

Criticism of other parties was expressed in only a few posts and included the accusation that the parties were only dealing with unimportant issues, as well as not acting in the interest of the European people. Despite fewer critical posts, the criticism expressed was clearly condemnatory: for example, by speaking of 'dirty tricks in the European Parliament' (ID group) in relation to the Ukraine war.

Regarding the tonality of the posts, both ID and ECR most frequently communicate neutrally (see table 6). While right-wing populist parties classically resort to strategies such as negative campaigning, the European groups and parties use a more moderate and less emotionalising communication style.

We also used automated sentiment analysis based on the AFINN dictionary to model the usage of positive and negative connoted words over the documents. A look at the frequency distribution of the AFINN sentiment scores (Figure 3) also shows that ID and ECR mostly write neutral comments. In addition, the majority of posts containing positively or negatively coded words are close to 0 (Q1 = 0, Q2 = 0, Q3 = 3, Min = -15, Max = 20, ⌀ = 01.05). In this respect, the moderate communication strategy of the parties is also reflected in the AFINN scores. To compare both parties regarding the absolute frequency of positive or negative coded words, we rescaled the original AFINN sentiment scores on a scale from -1 to 1 as described above. The differences between ID and ECR are statistically highly significant (at 0.001% level) with Chi² = 50.52 (df = 2). The effect is moderately strong with a Cramer's V of 0.29 (see Table 7). The recoded AFINN sentiments scores differ significantly from the manual coding, but still can be understood as an indicator for the frequency of positive and negative connoted words used by the parties. It is striking that ECR not only has a higher proportion of positive but also negative words within their posts than ID. However, an explanation for this is that ECR, with an average of 49.9 words, published posts with more than twice as many words per post as ID with an average of 24.0 words. Since longer texts increase the probability of positive and negative words occurring, we corrected the original AFINN scores for the word frequency of the posts. A comparison of the mean values of the two groups shows that ID with 0.07 now has a higher mean than ECR with 0.03 (Welch t = -02.46, df = 363.88, p = < 0.05, Cohen's d = 0.21). So, while in terms of absolute frequency ECR produces on average more posts that contain positive connoted words, ID uses more positive words relative to the word count. We also linked the sentiment scores from the manual analysis to the topics modelled in the STM. We were particularly interested in the immigration topic, as it also includes Ukrainian refugees. Migrants were mentioned in a total of 9.90% of all posts, of which 9.84% were positive, 47.54% negative and 42.62% neutral ones. Nonetheless, the positive posts cannot be equated with a positive portrayal of migrants. The manual analysis revealed that only three posts welcomed migrants, all of those referring to refugees from Ukraine.

Overall, ID was more critical of migrants than ECR which became visible in video content as well. Chega (ID) politician Andre Ventura, for example, used the phrase 'We will not allow our population to be replaced in Europe' in connection with migration. Thus, according to this example, migration not only poses a financial threat to the European population, but migrants are also accused of wanting to replace Europeans altogether, a far-right conspiracy theory (Ekman, 2022).

sections.paper_id: Peters and Schlußmeier - 2025 - Populist alone, but harmless together Analysing inter- and transnational populism of EP parties ID
paragraphs: The aim of this analysis was to examine whether ID and ECR use right-wing populist narratives and whether they differ from each other (RQ1 and RQ2). The results highlight inter-and transnational populism differing from national populism in certain aspects. Some of the postings of ID and ECR seem 'harmless' compared to their national member parties. At its core, even the modelled ID topic of 'elite blaming' appears to revolve around only one post about one singular politician (Emmanuel Macron) that has been reposted multiple times. ID and ECR also seem to frame their contributions neutral or rather positively compared to national parties, which often work with negative campaigning. These general distinctions might be explained by the specific design of the European multi-level system. The right-wing populist, European party alliances rather strive to increase their political impact at the European level through cooperation compared to their national member parties at the national level. Among other things, this seems to be related to the electoral decision being made at the national level. These forms of image construction clearly demonstrate how strategically and professionally European right-wing populist actors operate with regard to their communication strategies in social media. A closer look at the two parties indicates clear differences between ID and ECR in terms of right-wing populist communication strategies. While ID corresponds most closely to the communication of national member parties with topics such as migration and elite blaming, these issues are rarely addressed by ECR. Administrative topics and references to their own events are central to both parties. It seems that for ID and ECR the construction of an ingroup, the European population or the national population of different European countries, is communicatively of the biggest importance. The differences between ID and ECR can be explained by their party logic, that is, both right-wing populist party alliances strive to distinguish themselves from each other. While international cooperation is more important for ECR and sanctions against Russia are strongly favoured, ID is largely opposed to them. ECR in particular seems to increasingly aim for connectivity with the European political centre. At the political level, this is also reflected in joint activities, such as co-sponsored amendments by the ECR and EPP, with the EPP cooperating primarily with moderate MEPs of the ECR (Ripoll Servent, 2022). This study demonstrates that it is worthwhile for populism research to also focus on inter-and transnational parties. The extent of research on national parties indicates that they are considered to be more relevant than European parties. However, recent political developments make it clear that national right-wing populist parties are also influenced by the logics and strategies of the European parties and EP groups. For example, the AfD was excluded from the ID as a result of the scandal surrounding Maximillian Krah, the AfD's lead candidate for the European elections in May 2024. The reasons for this were Krah's uncritical, downplaying and trivialising statements about the SS crimes during World War II, which were considered too extreme by ID. This in turn led to Krah cancelling all election campaign appearances and stepping down from the federal board of the AfD. Despite these developments, the AfD came second in the 2024 European elections in Germany, still indicating that European political processes barely seem to matter to nationally organised voters.

Our aim was to make a first attempt at closing the research gap on European right-wing populist parties in political communication research. As it is of course limited, future research could specifically investigate aspects that were not part of our study. For example, we did not focus on how ID and ECR frame their election campaigns as they were not covered in the period we studied. Furthermore, we only observed party and parliamentary group accounts, but not party members or MEPs. It would be relevant to investigate whether there are differences between official party communication and MEPs. In addition, a larger study that directly examines the communication of EP parties and their national member parties in the same period would also be profitable. Moreover, the right-wing populist party spectrum at EU level has changed since the 2024 European elections. While the ECR group continues to exist, numerous member parties of the ID Group have joined the 'Patriots for Europe' initiated by the FPÖ, Fidesz and ANO, which is now the third largest group in the EU Parliament. The AfD is now the largest party in the newly founded 'Europe of Sovereign Nations' group. Here, further studies should examine how these newly formed parliamentary groups act communicatively and whether the changed party constellations lead to different communicative strategies. Finally, we can support our thesis that European parties and EP groups operate with a different logic than their national member parties only for their social media communication. Further studies should therefore also examine other data sources like party documents, legislative amendments and speeches. Our study is of course also subject to methodological limitations, which relate to the fact that we mainly applied automated methods like STM or sentiment analysis. There is also a need for studies that explore the questions raised in this paper using different quantitative and qualitative methods such as manual content analysis or qualitative interviews. In summary, we argue for a stronger focus on European party alliances when researching right-wing populism in the EU. This analysis has made clear that other logics are at work in contrast to the multitude of national parties, which need to be reflected in future research.
sections.text:
The aim of this analysis was to examine whether ID and ECR use right-wing populist narratives and whether they differ from each other (RQ1 and RQ2). The results highlight inter-and transnational populism differing from national populism in certain aspects. Some of the postings of ID and ECR seem 'harmless' compared to their national member parties. At its core, even the modelled ID topic of 'elite blaming' appears to revolve around only one post about one singular politician (Emmanuel Macron) that has been reposted multiple times. ID and ECR also seem to frame their contributions neutral or rather positively compared to national parties, which often work with negative campaigning. These general distinctions might be explained by the specific design of the European multi-level system. The right-wing populist, European party alliances rather strive to increase their political impact at the European level through cooperation compared to their national member parties at the national level. Among other things, this seems to be related to the electoral decision being made at the national level. These forms of image construction clearly demonstrate how strategically and professionally European right-wing populist actors operate with regard to their communication strategies in social media. A closer look at the two parties indicates clear differences between ID and ECR in terms of right-wing populist communication strategies. While ID corresponds most closely to the communication of national member parties with topics such as migration and elite blaming, these issues are rarely addressed by ECR. Administrative topics and references to their own events are central to both parties. It seems that for ID and ECR the construction of an ingroup, the European population or the national population of different European countries, is communicatively of the biggest importance. The differences between ID and ECR can be explained by their party logic, that is, both right-wing populist party alliances strive to distinguish themselves from each other. While international cooperation is more important for ECR and sanctions against Russia are strongly favoured, ID is largely opposed to them. ECR in particular seems to increasingly aim for connectivity with the European political centre. At the political level, this is also reflected in joint activities, such as co-sponsored amendments by the ECR and EPP, with the EPP cooperating primarily with moderate MEPs of the ECR (Ripoll Servent, 2022). This study demonstrates that it is worthwhile for populism research to also focus on inter-and transnational parties. The extent of research on national parties indicates that they are considered to be more relevant than European parties. However, recent political developments make it clear that national right-wing populist parties are also influenced by the logics and strategies of the European parties and EP groups. For example, the AfD was excluded from the ID as a result of the scandal surrounding Maximillian Krah, the AfD's lead candidate for the European elections in May 2024. The reasons for this were Krah's uncritical, downplaying and trivialising statements about the SS crimes during World War II, which were considered too extreme by ID. This in turn led to Krah cancelling all election campaign appearances and stepping down from the federal board of the AfD. Despite these developments, the AfD came second in the 2024 European elections in Germany, still indicating that European political processes barely seem to matter to nationally organised voters.

Our aim was to make a first attempt at closing the research gap on European right-wing populist parties in political communication research. As it is of course limited, future research could specifically investigate aspects that were not part of our study. For example, we did not focus on how ID and ECR frame their election campaigns as they were not covered in the period we studied. Furthermore, we only observed party and parliamentary group accounts, but not party members or MEPs. It would be relevant to investigate whether there are differences between official party communication and MEPs. In addition, a larger study that directly examines the communication of EP parties and their national member parties in the same period would also be profitable. Moreover, the right-wing populist party spectrum at EU level has changed since the 2024 European elections. While the ECR group continues to exist, numerous member parties of the ID Group have joined the 'Patriots for Europe' initiated by the FPÖ, Fidesz and ANO, which is now the third largest group in the EU Parliament. The AfD is now the largest party in the newly founded 'Europe of Sovereign Nations' group. Here, further studies should examine how these newly formed parliamentary groups act communicatively and whether the changed party constellations lead to different communicative strategies. Finally, we can support our thesis that European parties and EP groups operate with a different logic than their national member parties only for their social media communication. Further studies should therefore also examine other data sources like party documents, legislative amendments and speeches. Our study is of course also subject to methodological limitations, which relate to the fact that we mainly applied automated methods like STM or sentiment analysis. There is also a need for studies that explore the questions raised in this paper using different quantitative and qualitative methods such as manual content analysis or qualitative interviews. In summary, we argue for a stronger focus on European party alliances when researching right-wing populism in the EU. This analysis has made clear that other logics are at work in contrast to the multitude of national parties, which need to be reflected in future research.
